attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the gastroenterology outpatient clinic of a major tertiary hospital three months after an admission and new diagnosis of Crohn's disease. She initially presented four months ago with abdominal pain, diarrhoea and approximately 8 kg weight loss over the previous year. Investigations at the time of presentation showed:

  • C-reactive protein 6 mg/L (<5)

  • Faecal calprotectin 350 μg/g (<50)

A colonoscopy was performed which demonstrated an oedematous ileocaecal valve with ulceration of the valve and of the last 10 cm of the terminal ileum, beyond which a moderate stricture was found. Baseline magnetic resonance enterography (MRE) was performed and demonstrated inflammatory changes of the distal 10 cm of the terminal ileum adjacent to a 2cm inflammatory stricture, with no other inflammatory changes or strictures throughout the rest of the small bowel. She was commenced on corticosteroids followed by azathioprine maintenance therapy on discharge.

On current review, she has no ongoing gastrointestinal symptoms and her weight has stabilised. Repeat colonoscopy and faecal calprotectin is arranged.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following additional investigations is most appropriate to monitor her response to therapy long-term?
attributes.explanation:
A key element of inflammatory bowel disease management is the monitoring of disease activity and response to therapy. In Crohn's disease, a combination of clinical, endoscopic, imaging and biochemical parameters is used to monitor intestinal inflammation and complications.

Of the available options, point-of-care intestinal ultrasound is the most ideal investigation to support monitoring of disease activity in this patient. Intestinal ultrasound is increasingly being performed in IBD clinics, and is comparable to other cross-sectional imaging modalities (CT, MRI) in assessing IBD activity, including stricturing or penetrating complications in Crohn's disease. It enables real-time review of disease status, is non-invasive, does not expose patients to radiation or require bowel preparation, and can be useful in including patients in their management.

CT abdomen pelvis is not an appropriate investigation to monitor Crohn's disease activity. While it can be useful to assess complications such as abscesses, it is not a good modality for assessing small bowel inflammation. CT enterography would be more appropriate for this indication, however exposes this young woman to avoidable radiation.

Similarly, plain abdominal X-ray is not recommended as a means of monitoring IBD, being useful predominantly in acute, severe presentations of colitis.

Although serum C-reactive protein (CRP) is frequently ordered and can be helpful in some patients, it is not a reliable marker of intestinal inflammation. As this case demonstrates, a patient may have significant disease activity without a significant rise in inflammatory markers.

Capsule endoscopy can be useful in determining the extent of small bowel affected in Crohn's disease. However, it carries a risk of causing obstruction if small bowel strictures are present, such as in this patient. Cross-sectional imaging modalities are able to also assess the small bowel without this risk.

In practice, a combination of cross-sectional imaging (magnetic resonance enterography or intestinal ultrasound) and colonoscopy is typically used to monitor Crohn's disease activity in combination with markers such as faecal calprotectin. The choice between these modalities often depends on local expertise and availability and patient characteristics.
learningPoints: Cross-sectional imaging options (magnetic resonance enterography and intestinal ultrasound) combined with colonoscopy are used to both diagnose and monitor IBD activity.
Limiting life-time radiation exposure is a key consideration in long-term IBD management.
C-reactive protein is not a reliable marker of disease activity in IBD
references: Alex G, Andrews JM, Bell S, et al. Clinical update for general practitioners and physicians – inflammatory bowel disease. The Gastroenterological Society of Australia (GESA); Melbourne AU, 2018.
Radford SJ, Clarke C, Shinkins B, et al. Clinical utility of small bowel ultrasound assessment of Crohn’s disease in adults: a systematic scoping review. Frontline Gastroenterology 2022;13:280–286.
